posts.name: Paul Kaletsch
posts.cooked:
Hi,
I am currently on the hunt for some wine gear—thanks for your recs re a Durand emulation with an ah so and waiter’s corkscrew (will order soon!)—and since no dedicated thread seems to exist for this particular topic, here goes: I am interested in purchasing a steel funnel and sieve. A funnel with a detachable filter would be great, since I don’t always want to screen when I decant, or two separate products (filter and funnel). I want to use these tools either when I mess up decanting and did not get rid of all the sediment or when the opening damaged a cork and cork bits are in the wine. I am not worried about fine sediment. I do not care for cheesecloth/coffee filter solutions. I am not interested in products with an integrated additional aeration function. I’m based in Germany, so I appreciate recommendations of products that I can obtain here (or that I can ship from Europe). Thanks so much, hope that this is also helpful for others.
PS: Also looking for affordable dishwasher-safe decanters.

wmf.com

VINO Decanting funnel

The Vino Decanting Funnel by WMF makes decanting wine effortless. Crafted from sleek dishwasher-safe Cromargan®, it features an integrated sieve and fine holes at the outlet for refined wine that is perfectly aerated. Perfect for wine connoisseurs.

Price: EUR 32.99

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
Been using this one for a long while. Screen is separate. Does a great job.

IMG_22553024×4032 1.55 MB

posts.name: Luca Giupponi
posts.cooked: There are a ton on Amazon. I have this one and it works great.

posts.name: Tom R W  \/\/@rren
posts.cooked:
I have the one David mentioned & it works well for the big chunks.
When I want a finer screen I put something like this in.

https://www.amazon.com/Coffee-Paperless-Dripper-Reusable-Stainless/dp/B08GX2DZH2

posts.name: Paul @bbott
posts.cooked: I agree with this, my brother in law has spoiled some good bottles of burgundy because the fine sediment passes straight through a metal filter.

posts.name: Troy Morris
posts.cooked:
royalselangor.com

Wine Celebration Wine Funnel

Elegant fluting on the funnel’s gentle ogee profile leads the eye down to openings designed to launch wine softly against the decanter’s neck. Flowing down the decanter’s walls enhances aeration of the wine whilst cushioning it’s descent.Winner in...

Price: USD 130

My wife gave me this one as a gift years ago. I like it a lot, but if I had to replace it I wouldn’t spend that much!

posts.name: Paul Kaletsch
posts.cooked: Thanks so much, this looks perfect. Just one question: Does it have a special aeration feature (I don’t want that) or does it just work like a regular funnel (that’s what I’m looking for)?

posts.name: Al Osterheld
posts.cooked:
It’s not intended for aeration since it’s designed as a coffee filter. But, it works similarly to wine funnels that are intended for aeration and would have a similar effect since it will create many small streams of wine.

-Al

posts.name: Luca Giupponi
posts.cooked: You can remove both the fine and coarse sieve and it works like a regular funnel.

posts.name: Jim Stewart
posts.cooked: I try to remove the cork intact and decant carefully, but stuff happens so I will use something like what David posted for my cork bits, and a Melitta coffee filter for finer particles and missed sediment.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked: Note the cork floats. If you have a disintegrated cork mess and dont have a filter or, well, you don’t really need a filter for that. You need technique. It’s all floating in a narrow surface area in the neck. A quick, smooth tiny pour will take virtually all of that out in a quarter ounce wave.

posts.name: Paul Kaletsch
posts.cooked: Thank you, I bought this one

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked:
These work pretty well and are exceedingly cheap. But it’s a filter and funnel combined, and can clog easily if there’s a lot of sediment.
https://www.amazon.com/Calculi-Strainer-Collector-Straining-Analysis/dp/B0D31S6JGR/

posts.name: David E
posts.cooked: Does anyone else use neutral cheese cloth or coffee filter in conjunction with a metal funnel? I find this works pretty well.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: I have, but I find coffee filters to be too slow and often messy. Cheese cloth is just not consistent enough.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: My recommendation would be to also equip yourself with some seafood picks. It’s rare that, even if a cork breaks during extraction, that you can’t remove the residue with two seafood picks. And that obviates the need to pass it through a sieve.

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
Do not remember where I bought it and there’s no brand name, but this is a wonderful tool.

sediment strainer insert1920×2560 142 KB

If you are taking a wine with sediment to a BYO restaurant or to a a friend’s place, this is a great no-muss, no-fuss way to enjoy your bottle if it doesn’t have time to rest and be decanted.

posts.name: Paul Kaletsch
posts.cooked: Right, I forgot about those. Thanks for mentioning them
